title: Drape-moulded shallow dish set
subtitle: Three matching dishes formed by draping clay slabs over a household dome
excerpt: Three shallow dishes formed by draping rolled clay slabs over an upturned bowl or dome. No kiln required; air-dry clay takes the curve well and the slabs hold their shape once dry. The finished dishes are decorative pieces for keys, jewellery, or dried botanicals.
sourceNotes: Drape-mould slab forming is documented across early ceramic traditions and remains a standard hand-building method. V&A open-access collection for slab-formed shallow ware reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dome-shaped form that a clay slab is laid over to take a bowl shape. A household upturned bowl or a purpose-made plaster mould both work.
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A drape-moulded dish takes a shallow bowl shape by being draped over a curved former. The clay rests on the outside of the dome and follows its curve as it stiffens. Once the slab reaches leather-hard, the dish lifts off cleanly. This version uses three sizes of household bowl as the moulds and makes a graduated set. No kiln required; the dishes are decorative.

items.name: Three upturned bowls (about 18 cm, 14 cm, 10 cm across)
items.substitution: A dedicated bisque drape mould if available

items.name: Cling film or thin cotton cloth

content.text: Preparation

Wedge the clay for two or three minutes on the board. Cover each upturned bowl with a single layer of cling film or a thin cotton cloth: this creates a release barrier so the clay does not bond to the mould. A bisque-fired drape mould releases on its own and needs no covering.
Forming the dishes
1. Roll the slabs
Divide the clay into three pieces by weight: 300 g, 200 g, and 100 g for the three dishes. Pat each into a thick disc and roll between the guide rails to an even 6 mm thickness. Each rolled
slab
should overlap the corresponding mould by 30 mm all around to give a generous trimming margin.
2. Drape each slab

Lift the slab carefully on both hands and lay it across the upturned bowl. Smooth from the centre outward with flat hands or the wooden rib to settle the slab against the dome. Press gently; the clay drapes naturally over the curve. Avoid stretching, which leaves the wall thin at the top of the dome.
3. Trim the rim

Use the fettling knife to cut around the edge of the slab, following the outline of the bowl's rim. Hold the knife vertical so the cut is straight rather than bevelled. The trimmings go back into the bag for re-wedging. Run a damp sponge once around the cut edge to smooth and round it.
4. Set on the board to stiffen
Leave the dish on its
drape mould
for 4 to 8 hours, until the clay reaches
leather-hard
. The exact time depends on room temperature and humidity. Test with a fingertip on the inside of the rim: leather-hard clay feels firm but still cool, not tacky.
5. Lift and finish

Once leather-hard, gently lift the dish off the mould. Pull the cling film away. Turn the dish right-side-up and smooth the interior surface with the wooden rib if any film marks or wrinkles are visible. Sponge around the rim once more to finalise the edge.
Drying

Set the dishes upright on the board to finish drying. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 12 hours so the interior and exterior dry at similar rates: open clay shrinks unevenly and warps. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes one to two days.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the rim and the underside with 220-grit sandpaper. Seal inside and out with two coats of PVA or acrylic varnish. Let the first coat dry fully before the second. Paint with acrylics before sealing if a colour finish is wanted.
Care

Air-dry dishes are not waterproof and not food-safe. Use them for keys, jewellery, paperclips, a small cluster of dried flowers, or any other dry household contents. Wipe with a damp cloth; do not submerge.
Variations
Press lace, a leaf, or a fabric texture into the slab before draping for an impressed pattern across the outside of the finished dish.
Use a square or oval bowl as the mould for a non-circular drape.
Pinch a small foot ring into the underside while the dish is still leather-hard: a coil of clay pressed in a circle on the base and smoothed in.

items.symptom: Dish cracks across the base as it dries
items.cause: Slab was stretched too thin over the centre of the dome, or dried too fast
items.fix: Roll the slab thicker (a full 6 mm) and lay it without stretching; cover loosely with plastic for the first 12 hours

items.symptom: Dish bonds to the mould and will not lift off
items.cause: Mould was not covered with cling film or cloth
items.fix: Always cover a non-bisque mould before draping; if stuck, gently lift one section at a time with a thin palette knife

items.cause: Rim dried faster than the body of the dish
items.fix: Cover with plastic for the first 12 hours; sponge the rim again before lifting off the mould to soften any small cracks already forming

items.symptom: Dish warps to one side after lifting
items.cause: Lifted off the mould too early, before reaching leather-hard
items.fix: Leave the dish on the mould a further hour or two; press the warp gently back into shape while still slightly damp and re-dry flat
